Swiss CitiesEngelberg

Engelberg

OWObwalden

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The angels on the Hahnen

A child accompanies Count Konrad von Sellenbueren into the wild alpine valley in 1120, where snow-capped peaks tower on all sides. As they search for the perfect place to build a monastery, angelic voices ring out from the summit of the Hahnen, and the child sees a choir of shimmering figures in the sky -- giving the valley its name forever.

Create This Story

On 1 April 1120, Count Konrad von Sellenbueren, a Zurich-area nobleman, founded the Benedictine abbey at Engelberg. The name 'Engelberg' means 'Mountain of Angels' (Engel = angel, Berg = mountain). According to legend, when Konrad and his companions arrived in the high alpine valley, they heard angelic voices singing and saw a choir of angels in the sky above the Hahnen (2,606 m), the distinctive mountain overlooking the monastery site. The first abbot Adelhelm came from Muri Abbey. Konrad entered the monastery himself after founding it, and was murdered on 2 May 1126 during a property dispute. He is venerated locally as a martyr of justice.

The revolving gondola

A child rides the Rotair to the summit of Titlis, and as the cabin slowly rotates 360 degrees, each window reveals a different century of Engelberg's history -- monks building the monastery in 1120, the great fire of 1729, the first skiers of 1904, and the shimmering angels that gave the valley its name.

Create This Story

The Titlis Rotair, inaugurated 20 December 1992, is the world's first revolving aerial cable car -- the gondola completes one full 360-degree rotation during the five-minute journey from Stand station (2,428 m) to Klein Titlis (3,020 m), carrying up to 125 passengers. Mount Titlis stands at 3,238 meters, the highest peak in central Switzerland. The name derives from a local farmer called Tutilos, documented as 'Tuttelsberg' in 1435. The Titlis cableway system expanded steadily: Gerschnialp-Truebsee (1927), Truebsee-Jochpass chairlift (1943), Truebsee-Stand (1965-1967), Rotair to Klein Titlis (1992). The glacier cave accessible from Klein Titlis penetrates the approximately 5,000-year-old heart of the Titlis glacier.

The secret of the scriptorium

A medieval manuscript from Abbot Frowin's time is missing its last page. A child follows clues left by the anonymous Engelberg Master through the monastery library, the cloister garden, and the Baroque church, searching for what the master illustrated in brown and red ink eight centuries ago -- and why he hid it.

Create This Story

In the mid-12th century, Abbot Frowin (1143-1178) founded a scriptorium at Engelberg that became one of the most productive in medieval Switzerland. He was the first abbot to rely on domestic manuscript production with an internal scribal workforce. The anonymous 'Engelberg Master' (active around 1200) created extraordinarily artful initials in brown and red ink -- his work survives in multiple codices. The library grew to over 20,000 volumes and 200 choice manuscripts before being pillaged by French troops in 1798. Today, approximately 290 medieval manuscripts survive in the Stiftsbibliothek, dating from the 7th to 17th century, many digitized through the e-codices virtual manuscript library of Switzerland.

The Klosterglocke that rang by itself

The monastery's famous cheese shares its name with a bell -- the Klosterglocke. One night, the dairy bell rings on its own, and a child discovers that the monks' 900-year-old recipe is calling for a special ingredient that can only be found on the high alpine pastures above Engelberg, where the freshest mountain milk holds a secret.

Create This Story

The 'Chaes im Kloster' (Cheese in the Monastery) is a show dairy and cheese shop located within the old monastery walls of Engelberg. Cheese has been made in the valley for almost 900 years. The signature product is the Engelberger Klosterglocke ('monastery bell'), a soft cream cheese produced using fresh mountain milk from 15 local dairy farmers. The dairy operates four vats in continuous production using a traditional method. In addition to the Klosterglocke, the dairy produces goat and sheep cheeses. Visitors can watch production and participate in hands-on cheese-making workshops. The monastery church houses Switzerland's largest organ with 9,097 sounding pipes and 137 stops.

Crossing the Surenen Pass

A child must carry an urgent message from Engelberg over the ancient Surenen Pass to Canton Uri. Along the way, they encounter a marmot family standing guard, a glacier stream to ford, and the ghost of Konrad von Sellenbueren at the summit -- who reminds them that the mountains reward those brave enough to cross them.

Create This Story

The Surenen Pass (2,291 m) connects the upper Engelberg valley eastward to the Urner Reusstal (Canton Uri), an ancient crossing route with views of the Chli Spannort (3,140 m) and Gross Spannort (3,198 m). Engelberg lies at 1,000 meters above sea level in the Canton of Obwalden. The valley is drained by the Engelberger Aa, which flows into Lake Lucerne. Surrounded by an amphitheater of peaks including Titlis (3,238 m), Hahnen (2,606 m), Wissberg (2,627 m), and Engelberger Rotstock (2,819 m). The valley's enclosed geography -- accessible only through a narrow gorge from the north -- reinforced the monastery's centuries-long isolation. Count Konrad von Sellenbueren, the monastery's founder, was murdered on 2 May 1126 during a property dispute.

The bridge above the clouds

A nervous child steps onto the Cliff Walk at 3,000 meters and discovers that the one-meter-wide bridge connects two worlds: the snowy summit where the Ice Flyer soars over crevasses twenty meters deep, and an underground glacier cave five thousand years old. Halfway across, the clouds part to reveal all of Engelberg far below.

Create This Story

The Titlis Cliff Walk, opened 7 December 2012, is Europe's highest suspension bridge at approximately 3,000 meters elevation -- spanning 100 meters in length but only 1 meter wide, constructed in five months over a sheer cliff face. The Ice Flyer chairlift, opened in 1999, carries visitors over the glacier, offering views into crevasses up to 20 meters deep; the ride covers 680 meters in just over two minutes. The glacier cave, accessible from the Klein Titlis cable car station, penetrates the approximately 5,000-year-old heart of the Titlis glacier. The first ski ascent of Titlis was accomplished on 21 January 1904 by Joseph Kuster and Willi Amrhein.

Little Versailles in the mountains

A child in 1905 explores the grand hotels of Engelberg's Belle Epoque: the Kursaal's music room with its crystal chandeliers, the electric railway that arrived before Zurich had power, and the mysterious cure doctor who prescribes nothing but mountain air. In the lobby of 'Little Versailles,' they meet guests from across Europe who have come to be healed by the alpine valley.

Create This Story

In the 1840s, physician Carl Cattani established Engelberg as a health resort, promoting mountain air, mineral water, and bathing cures. In 1865, Eduard Cattani opened the Hotel Titlis, Engelberg's first luxury hotel. The semicircle of the Hotel Titlis, Grand Hotel Winterhaus (opened 1905), Kursaal (1902), and Kuranstalt (1899) earned the nickname 'Little Versailles.' In 1898, the railway connection opened -- the longest electric railway line in Switzerland at the time. In 1911, Engelberg recorded 165,922 visitor-nights. Winter tourism began in 1903-1904, and the first hydroelectric plant in the late 1880s brought electric power to Engelberg before Zurich had it.

The waterfall's song

The Taetschbach waterfall stops flowing in midsummer, and the valley begins to dry. A child hikes up to the Griessen glacier on the Wissigstock at 2,887 meters and discovers why the meltwater has stopped -- the glacier is retreating. The child must convince the ancient ice to release its water one last time before the Engelberger Aa runs dry.

Create This Story

The Taetschbachfall is one of the tallest waterfalls in Switzerland, located near the Furenalp cable car station in Engelberg. The total cascade is approximately 300 meters in height, with the lower single-drop section falling 60 meters. The waterfall is fed by meltwater from the Griessen glacier on the Wissigstock (2,887 m). The Taetschbach flows into the Engelberger Aa, the main river of the valley. Best visited in early summer when snowmelt maximizes water volume. Trubsee is an alpine lake at 1,764 meters filled with emerald-green glacier water, accessible by cable car -- the Gerschnialp-Truebsee aerial cableway opened 23 December 1927, Switzerland's first aerial cableway of this type.
